title: Earth’s Climate 'Flashing Red' as Historic Energy Imbalance Meets Looming El Niño
description: The UN's weather agency reports Earth's climate is experiencing record energy imbalances, warning that a looming El Niño could drive global temperatures to unprecedented heights.
lede: The Earth's climate is further out of balance than at any time in recorded history, the UN’s weather agency warns. Driven by record energy imbalances and fueled by greenhouse gas emissions, an impending El Niño threatens to push global temperatures to unprecedented new heights.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) has issued a dire warning that the planet is absorbing significantly more heat energy than it can release. This "energy imbalance," primarily driven by carbon dioxide emissions, is responsible for heating oceans to record levels and accelerating the melting of ice caps.

According to the WMO, the last 11 years represent the Earth's warmest period since records began in 1850. In 2025, global average air temperatures reached approximately 1.43C above pre-industrial levels, narrowly avoiding new absolute records only due to a temporary cooling effect from the La Niña weather pattern.

UN Secretary General António Guterres delivered a sharp response to the findings, stating, "Planet Earth is being pushed beyond its limits. Every key climate indicator is flashing red." He reiterated urgent calls for an aggressive transition from fossil fuels to renewable energy to secure global stability.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The consequences of this trapped energy are immediate and devastating. The WMO reports that more than 90% of the Earth's excess energy is absorbed by the oceans. The heat stored in the upper 2km of the global ocean reached a new high last year, warming at more than double the rate seen in the late 20th century.

This oceanic warming has profound knock-on effects, devastating marine ecosystems, driving more intense storm systems, and directly contributing to sea-level rise. Simultaneously, provisional data indicates that the world's glaciers experienced one of their five worst years on record in 2024/25, with polar sea ice hovering near record lows.

The impacts of these rising temperatures are already visible on land. The south-west United States is currently enduring a record-breaking early-season heatwave, with temperatures exceeding 40C—roughly 10-15C above the seasonal average. A rapid analysis by the World Weather Attribution group concluded that such intensity would have been "virtually impossible" without human-caused climate change.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: The root cause of this historic imbalance is unambiguous. The WMO confirms that atmospheric levels of carbon dioxide are currently at their highest point in at least two million years, directly resulting from human activities such as fossil fuel combustion.

Prof Celeste Saulo, secretary general of the WMO, emphasized the long-term reality of the crisis. "Human activities are increasingly disrupting the natural equilibrium and we will live with these consequences for hundreds and thousands of years," she stated.
The rapid environmental changes are also exacerbating public health crises by facilitating the spread of diseases such as dengue, alongside intensifying various forms of extreme weather globally.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: Climate researchers are now closely monitoring the Pacific Ocean, where long-term forecasts strongly suggest the formation of a warming El Niño phase in the second half of 2026.
The arrival of El Niño, compounded by the existing background trend of human-caused warming, threatens to shatter current temperature records into 2027.

"If we transition to El Niño we will see an increase in global temperature again, and potentially to new records," warned Dr John Kennedy of the WMO. The convergence of these factors signals an impending period of unprecedented climatic stress.
